Strait of Hormuz over the last 24 hours: another "calm before the storm" moment?

On the escalation front, some of the OSINT accounts pointed out that the recent attacks point to a ground operation. I have no idea on that front, so I will leave the experts to it.

But from a flow perspective, we are back to the start. Kpler has done a good job tracking shadow transits, and even though the data might not get revised for a few days, it is obvious the slowdown is coming. The last 2 days, flows were 0.074 and 0.198 million bbls. The previous 5 days were 5.516 million b/d. This brings the weekly average to 3.98 million b/d.

Looking at the video from @MarineTraffic, you can see that it's about as quiet as quiet gets. Iranian flows will also slow to nothing as the US naval blockade is in place.

With the recent US/Iran escalation moving to civilian targets, who knows where we go from here...

tweets.text:
that’s like 6 consecutive Black Friday’s, which is a 36σ event 

a one-tailed 36σ event has probability ≈ 4×10⁻²⁸⁴, or about 1 in 2.4×10²⁸³

To calibrate how absurd that number is: there are ~10⁸⁰ atoms in the observable universe, and ~10⁶⁰ Planck times have elapsed since the Big Bang. If every atom drew one sample every Planck time for the entire age of the universe, you’d have ~10¹⁴¹ draws and the odds of seeing even one 36σ outcome would still be around 1 in 10¹⁴².

Which is the real takeaway when this comes up in markets: observing a “36 sigma move” is evidence of a cosmically rare event. Would Gauss lie?

Chinese oil traders are some of the best at playing this game. They know they are the swing buyers and they can move as a group, unlike US or European traders.

Since sanctioned barrels can only go to China, they can actively sell barrels in the window to give the appearance of oversupply, and turn around and buy the sanctioned barrels at a discount.

Crude is oversupplied, as the market keeps on saying. So they know they have some time on their hands. By the time they want the market to know they want every barrel on the market, they will have already positioned for it.

You don't know what China is doing until after China is doing it.

thread_tweets.text:
UBS 2027 Industry Revenue = $1760B, dram under supplied until mid 28 but SKH/samsung fabs ramp 2H27, micron Idaho online mid-27 so moderate growth from 27-28, put it at 8% base case = $1900B

Attribution rate, CLSA has memory as a % of hyperscaler capex at 48% for 2027, implied attribution of 30% of memory industry revenue that lands in hyperscaler capex. Mix shift continues as supplier mix shifts to HBM/server DDR5/eSSD, extrapolating attribution at 37% 2028e. ~704B hyperscaler attributed memory spend.

Let’s say ~$1200B hyperscaler capex for 2028. That’s about 9% growth from 2027.

704/1200=0.587 

You can play around with memory growth / mix premium / capex growth, I think unless you have a really out of consensus estimate on any of those you’re landing between 55-60%.

The S&P 500 is up +95% since the end of 2022, placing the current bull market within the strongest 10% at this stage of the cycle, in data going back to 1928.

The current bull run has remained within the strongest 10% of historical bull markets for 2 years, excluding the March-April 2025 correction.

Since the April 2025 low alone, the S&P 500 has surged +51%.

Market momentum is incredibly strong.

tweets.text:
The Ethereum Foundation’s institutional privacy team just spun out into its own company.

This just went on my list of high-potential projects because the team behind it is unusually experienced for an early-stage privacy project.

They spent the past year working with banks, regulators, and asset managers through the Institutional Privacy Task Force, so they have undeniable experience in this sector.

Now they’re taking that work full-time under a for-profit company led by @oskarth.

Their research covers:

→ Private stablecoin transfers
→ Private bonds and settlement
→ Identity systems compatible with KYC

None of this is a finished privacy network yet. Their main goal is to turn those prototypes into systems institutions can use in production.

Btw, Bitmine + SharpLink + Joe Lubin backed the spinout.

A credible team with serious backers and a clear area of focus. I’m curious to see what they build for private finance on Ethereum.

tweets.text:
New research from @dune and @1inch found that 85% of DeFi liquidity is underutilized at any given time.

That's up to $1.6B in deposits that are not earning effectively.

Reasons are pretty straightforward:

• Ranges are set too wide
• LP positions move out of range and stop earning fees
• No one can monitor and rebalance positions around the clock manually

The research tracked concentrated liquidity for 26 weeks across 4 DEXs and 7 chains.

On average, 29.5% of it was out of range at any given time. That translates to an estimated $185M to $195M in missed fees each year, based only on the capital deployed today.

It’s hard to take talk of "bringing trillions onchain" seriously while so much of DeFi’s existing capital remains underutilized.

The next stage of DeFi growth depends on making existing liquidity work more efficiently before bringing more of it onchain.

Then it can scale from there.

tweets.text:
One of the hardest edges to build in AI is the data behind it.

Revenue at the top labs is climbing steeply, but the cost of running the models continues to fall.

As those costs fall, the real bottleneck becomes verification. The value moves to the data and training environments where truth is hard and expensive to establish.

This favors fields where reality is hard to label like biology and robotics. The data and environments there are scarce and expensive to build. That scarcity could compound into an advantage over time.

(Original content provided by @PonderingDurian at Delphi Ventures)

thread_tweets.text:
youre barking up the wrong tree. 

first of all, most DCs are air cooled. 

second, DCs that use water for cooling generally do it in a closed loop system. non evaporative. 

if you really wanted to complain about water, you would look at the wider scope, which is to say the water usage associated with the _power_ that the DCs are consuming. (not the datacenter per se) 

when you look at a combined cycle gas plant for instance, evaporative is not the only option. in plenty of cases you would use once-through cooling, meaning water is withdrawn from a river, lake or the sea, passes through a condenser, then gets returned. the water isn't lost. it's reinjected into the body of water at a higher temperature. 

yes, evaporative cooling happens. but you can use any kind of water for it, even seawater. it's common to use municipal wastewater. or even brackish groundwater or industrial wastewater. the water quality really doesn't matter. so no youre typically not depriving some municipality of their treated potable water. power plants are often using water that has no other purpose, because it's only being used as a heat sink.

"Most people who need a transplant die without getting one."

Nobel laureate Alvin Roth helped pioneer modern market design — the same toolkit crypto builders use to reason about rules, incentives, and allocation in onchain markets.

Here’s what that thinking looks like when applied to kidneys:

"You can't pay a donor for a kidney. That's a repugnant transaction... Kidneys are gifts. Their price is zero and they're very scarce therefore."

"Maybe I could give a kidney to your kid and you could give a kidney to my kid. That's a kidney exchange. Now we do about a third of living donor transplants through exchange."
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
First Principles Ep. 4 with Alvin Roth

Long before crypto made coordination programmable, Nobel Prize winner Alvin Roth was designing markets where coordination could save lives, matching doctors to hospitals, students to schools, and kidney donors to the patients who need them. https://t.co/qgl7sx4wo9

tweets.text: In the five‑step loop I teach, “diagnose” is the pivot point that turns a vague problem into a concrete lever for improvement. When you stop at step 2 and only name the symptom—say, “our portfolio underperformed this quarter”—you’re still not seeing the underlying cause. Sit with the discomfort long enough to ask: Is the issue a missing skill in our research team, a mis‑aligned incentive structure, or perhaps an external shock we didn’t anticipate? Use radical transparency and thoughtful disagreement to pull in other perspectives; remember that the best answer often lies outside your own head. Once you have a clear root cause, you can design a solution that hits the real source rather than just patching the surface. If the diagnosis reveals something you can’t fix yourself, escalate it or seek help—that’s part of the “best answer” principle. The key is to be specific about what you’re diagnosing, then iterate until the fix sticks. What kind of problems do you typically spot but hesitate to dig into deeply, and how might you bring in others to help you see the root cause?
tweets.full_text: In the five‑step loop I teach, “diagnose” is the pivot point that turns a vague problem into a concrete lever for improvement. When you stop at step 2 and only name the symptom—say, “our portfolio underperformed this quarter”—you’re still not seeing the underlying cause. Sit with the discomfort long enough to ask: Is the issue a missing skill in our research team, a mis‑aligned incentive structure, or perhaps an external shock we didn’t anticipate? Use radical transparency and thoughtful disagreement to pull in other perspectives; remember that the best answer often lies outside your own head. Once you have a clear root cause, you can design a solution that hits the real source rather than just patching the surface. If the diagnosis reveals something you can’t fix yourself, escalate it or seek help—that’s part of the “best answer” principle. The key is to be specific about what you’re diagnosing, then iterate until the fix sticks. What kind of problems do you typically spot but hesitate to dig into deeply, and how might you bring in others to help you see the root cause?

tweets.text:
You should advocate for Strategy to stop guaranteeing an ATM hit at $100. It's extremely bad for the product to automatically ATM it at $100, it offers the shorts a quasi zero downside trade while preventing all buyers from getting short-term upside above $100, and it incentivizes all the 'arb to par' dip buyers to frontrun the $100 level on their exit.

Also expecting the instrument to trade in a 1% range with such a volatile collateral is mathematically impossible. Think about it: $STRF regularly moves +/-10% in one month, which means it's the volatility you can expect with a stable dividend rate pref. $STRC is different in theory because the div rate instead of the price should be the adjustment variable. But the div rate changes very slowly (once at month and only by 50bps max), so over a one month timeframe, we should expect similar volatility for STRC as for STRF, meaning at least a 10% range in the more extreme months.

(The only way to suppress the volatility of STRC and make it trade in a $99-$100 range would be to massively restrict its mcap, which is clearly not the goal of Strategy.)

I think Strategy should state that they will only hit the ATM between $100 and $105 at their discretion from now on.
In the very short-term, it wouldn't solve the issue of STRC trading significantly below par, but it would be a step in the right direction and incentivize more buying.

I'm pretty certain that as a former engineering student, you will end up being convinced of the impossibility of having both a stable price (1% range) and a stable div rate (on short timeframe) on a pref backed by a very volatile collateral (BTC).

It's extremely bad for the product to automatically ATM it at $100, it offers the shorts a quasi zero downside trade while preventing all buyers from getting short-term upside above $100, and it incentivizes all the 'arb to par' dip buyers to frontrun the $100 level on their exit.

Also expecting the instrument to trade in a 1% range with such a volatile collateral is mathematically impossible. Think about it: $STRF regularly moves +/-10% in one month, which means it's the volatility you can expect with a stable dividend rate pref. $STRC is different in theory because the div rate instead of the price should be the adjustment variable. But the div rate changes very slowly (once at month and only by 50bps max), so over a one month timeframe, we should expect similar volatility for STRC as for STRF, meaning at least a 10% range in the more extreme months.

(The only way to suppress the volatility of STRC and make it trade in a $99-$100 range would be to massively restrict its mcap, which is clearly not the goal of Strategy.)

I think Strategy should state that they will only hit the ATM between $100 and $105 at their discretion from now on.
In the very short-term, it wouldn't solve the issue of STRC trading significantly below par, but it would be a step in the right direction and incentivize more buying.

I'm pretty certain that as a former engineering student, you will end up being convinced of the impossibility of having both a stable price (1% range) and a stable div rate (on short timeframe) on a pref backed by a very volatile collateral (BTC).

If Strategy truly believes that BTC will compound at 20%-30% CAGR, then they should be very happy to have 'secured' that leverage already, and there's no reason to buy STRC back.

The trust in STRC being pegged at $100 has been broken anyway now, it won't be fixed instantly by selling BTC for STRC here, which would be running the exact opposite trade as in May (with the goal of... then selling STRC at $100 to buy BTC again?)

thread_tweets.text:
It's actually very different, because the goal is not to have it trade at $105, it's to have it trade around $100, which you won't have if you strictly cap it at $100 at the same time.
Basically people like you shorting at $105 would be "useful" because they would be making sure that the instrument trades correctly (ie it reverts back to $100), whereas in the current setup, people shorting at $100 (and people selling at $100) are making the instrument trades strictly below $100 on average. If we expect a roughly 10% range, then capping the upside at $100 means STRC will likely trade around $95 on average, while Saylor wants the average to be less than 1% away from $100
